title: The Foundations Ladder
excerpt: Six rungs from the ground up: what data is, what analysis does, and where analytics actually sits. Built and cold-walked in one session.

questions.prompt: At the very bottom of the ladder: what is data?
options: A chart or graph that explains something
Any conclusion you draw from research
questions.explanation: Data = a collection of facts. One fact is a single true thing ("it rained Tuesday"); pile facts together and you have data. A chart is an artifact built FROM data, not data itself.
questions.hint: It's the rawest thing on the ladder — before any chart, conclusion, or insight exists.

questions.prompt: Data analysis turns data into ___.
questions.explanation: Analysis turns data into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself ("long hiring processes cause new hires to quit"). An artifact (a chart, a report) can carry an insight, but the insight is the point. Decisions come after the insight.
questions.hint: An artifact only carries it. The thing itself is the understanding.

questions.prompt: The everyday mechanism of analysis, in order:
questions.explanation: Pattern → Relationship → Prediction → Action. The first move is spotting a PATTERN — not Process (that lives up in the six-step lifecycle). Watch the two-P trap.
questions.hint: Pure Reform Pushes Action. First P = Pattern, NOT Process.

questions.prompt: Data science is the umbrella. Which three disciplines sit under it?
questions.explanation: Data science = "the discipline of making data useful," with three children: Statistics, Machine Learning/AI, and Analytics. (LLMs aren't a co-equal bucket — they're one tool inside ML.)
questions.hint: One of them is the bucket that an LLM lives INSIDE of.

questions.prompt: The three are split by how many decisions you know you want to make up front. Which one is "I don't know yet — I'm exploring"?
questions.explanation: Analytics = you DON'T know how many decisions yet; you're exploring for inspiration, hunting unknown unknowns. The analyst is the explorer/detective. (Statistics = a FEW known decisions; ML = MANY, automated.)
questions.hint: Explorer = don't-know. "Here's data nobody's looked at — go find something."

questions.prompt: In Cassie's split, statistics is for making ___ decisions under uncertainty.
questions.explanation: Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty, made carefully (its excellence is rigor — the careful philosopher). Many automated decisions = Machine Learning.
questions.hint: The careful philosopher protecting a small number of high-stakes calls.

questions.prompt: In Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act, which step comes immediately BEFORE Process?
questions.explanation: Prepare comes before Process — you plan (Prepare) before you clean (Process). Your mnemonic encodes it: PUPPIES (Prepare) before PICK (Process).
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally. PUPPIES before PICK.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the Process step (not Analyze)?
options: Clean the data and handle it ethically — consent, restrict access, store securely
Run the data through lenses to find the insights
Share the findings with managers in the right context
Define the business question and what success looks like
questions.explanation: Process = wash and prep: clean the data, get consent, restrict access, aggregate to protect individuals, store securely. Finding the insights is Analyze (the cooking). Defining the question is Ask; sharing is Share.
questions.hint: Food: Process = wash & prep the ingredients. Analyze = cook.

questions.prompt: Diagnostic analytics answers which question?
questions.explanation: Descriptive = what happened · Diagnostic = WHY it happened · Predictive = what will happen · Prescriptive = what should we do. They climb: what → why → what-next → what-to-do.
questions.hint: It's the doctor's word — diagnosing the cause.

questions.prompt: You want the analysis to recommend the best action to take. Which type?
questions.explanation: Prescriptive = what should we do — it prescribes the best action (like a prescription). Predictive only forecasts what will happen; it doesn't tell you what to do about it.
questions.hint: Like a prescription from a doctor — it tells you what to DO.

questions.prompt: In your own words: what does data analysis do to data, and what's the difference between an insight and an artifact?
questions.answer: Data analysis turns data (a collection of facts) into INSIGHTS — the understanding itself, e.g. "long, complicated hiring makes new hires quit." An ARTIFACT (a chart, a report) is just a thing that carries the insight; the insight is the point, the artifact is the delivery.
questions.explanation: The key distinction you sharpened in E27: analysis produces understanding (insight), not just objects (artifacts). Artifacts only carry insights.
questions.hint: One is the understanding itself; the other just carries it.

questions.prompt: From memory, name the six steps of the data analysis process — in order.
questions.answer: Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act
questions.explanation: The spine of the whole certificate. Prepare before Process (plan before clean); Analyze before Share before Act.
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally.

questions.prompt: Explain what separates statistics, machine learning, and analytics in Cassie's map.
questions.answer: How many decisions you know you want to make before you begin. Statistics = a FEW important decisions under uncertainty (rigor). Machine learning = MANY decisions, automated, under uncertainty (performance). Analytics = you DON'T know how many yet — you're exploring for inspiration (speed). All three sit under the data-science umbrella.
questions.explanation: The dividing axis is the number of decisions known up front — the framing that finally untied the analytics-vs-science knot.
questions.hint: It's about how many decisions you know about up front.

title: The Data Ecosystem
excerpt: Where data lives and the words that get mixed up: the six-verb ecosystem, the cloud, analysis vs. analytics, scientist vs. analyst, and when to trust your gut. Five question types, trap-spots first.

questions.prompt: What is a data ecosystem?
options: The group of elements — hardware, software, and the people who use them — that interact to produce, manage, store, organize, analyze, and share data
A single database where a company keeps all its records
The cloud servers that store a company's data online
The team of data analysts inside a company
questions.explanation: A data ecosystem = various elements (hardware + software tools + the people who use them) interacting to produce, manage, store, organize, analyze, and share data. It's the whole environment data lives in — not one database, not just the cloud, not just people.
questions.hint: Think 'environment data lives in' — tools AND people, doing six things.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: a data ecosystem's elements interact to produce, manage, store, ___, analyze, and share data.
questions.explanation: Produce · Manage · Store · ORGANIZE · Analyze · Share. Your mnemonic: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar (P-M-S-O-A-S). 'Organize' is the O.
questions.hint: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar — what's the O?

questions.prompt: Select ALL six things a data ecosystem's elements interact to do with data.
questions.explanation: The six: Produce, Manage, Store, Organize, Analyze, Share (P-M-S-O-A-S). Delete, Sell, and Encrypt are not on the list — watch the plausible distractors.
questions.hint: Penguins Make Soggy Omelettes And Sugar — six words, six verbs.

questions.prompt: True or False: 'the cloud' means storing data on your own computer's hard drive.
questions.explanation: False. The cloud is the opposite — data kept ONLINE and accessed over the internet, rather than on a local hard drive or inside your own network. It's a virtual location.
questions.hint: Cloud = online / over the internet, not on your machine.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: the cloud is a place to keep data ___, rather than on a computer hard drive.
questions.explanation: The cloud keeps data ONLINE — accessed over the internet instead of stored locally. It's just a term for the virtual location.
questions.hint: The opposite of 'on a hard drive' — where data is reached over the internet.

questions.prompt: Which term does this course define as 'the science of data' — the broad umbrella over data, data analysis, and the ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Data ANALYTICS = 'the science of data' — the broad umbrella (in this course's framing) over data, data analysis, and the ecosystem. Data analysis is the hands-on activity inside it. (Cassie's video made data SCIENCE the umbrella — different author; for THIS course, analytics is the umbrella.)
questions.hint: The '-ics' word — the big tent, the science of data.

questions.prompt: True or False: data analysis is the broad umbrella, and data analytics is the hands-on activity.
questions.explanation: False — it's reversed. Data ANALYSIS is the hands-on activity (collect, transform, organize → conclusions). Data ANALYTICS is the umbrella, 'the science of data.' analy-SIS = the step you do; analy-TICS = the big tent.
questions.hint: Which suffix is the small hands-on one? -sis or -tics?

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between a data scientist and a data analyst?
options: Scientists create new questions using data; analysts find answers to existing questions
Analysts create new questions; scientists answer the existing ones
They do the same job under different titles
Scientists clean the data; analysts build the dashboards
questions.explanation: Data scientists CREATE NEW QUESTIONS using data (the explorer — 'what should we even ask?'). Data analysts FIND ANSWERS to existing questions by creating insights. The hinge word on both sides is questions.
questions.hint: One makes the questions, one answers them. Which way round?

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: data scientists create new ___ using data, while analysts find answers to existing ones.
questions.explanation: QUESTIONS — the same word fills both sides. Scientists make new questions; analysts answer existing questions. (Not 'artifacts' — that's the word we killed at E27. Analysis makes insights, not artifacts.)
questions.hint: Same word both sides — and it is NOT 'artifacts.'

questions.prompt: Arrange the six steps of the data analysis process in the correct order.
questions.explanation: Ask → Prepare → Process → Analyze → Share → Act. Mnemonic: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally. Prepare BEFORE Process — you plan/collect before you clean.
questions.hint: All Puppies Pick Another Short Ally.

questions.prompt: Data-driven decision-making is defined as using ___ to guide business strategy.
questions.explanation: DDDM = using FACTS to guide business strategy. The other options matter (experience and intuition help) — but the definition itself is fact-led.
questions.hint: The definition's exact word — the thing data is made of.

questions.prompt: Why include subject-matter experts when building data into business strategy? Select all that apply.
options: Their experience and intuition add value to data-driven decisions
They're familiar with the business problem
They can review the results and spot inconsistencies or make sense of gray areas
They are the ones responsible for finding and analyzing the data
questions.explanation: SMEs bring human experience/intuition, know the business problem, and validate results / catch inconsistencies / make sense of gray areas. The last option is the ANALYST'S job, not the SME's — that's the trap.
questions.hint: Three are about business judgment; one is actually the analyst's job.

questions.prompt: True or False: data on its own is more powerful than data combined with human experience and intuition.
questions.explanation: False. The video is explicit: 'data alone will never be as powerful as data combined with human experience, observation, and sometimes even intuition.' Data + people beats data alone.
questions.hint: Data + experience > data alone. Always.

questions.prompt: Fill the blank: gut instinct is an intuitive understanding of something with little or no ___.
questions.explanation: Gut instinct = an intuitive understanding with little or no EXPLANATION — you 'just know,' often picking up signals without realizing it.
questions.hint: You can't ___ why you know it — you just do.

questions.prompt: The 'Data and gut instinct' reading says the right mix of data vs. gut depends mostly on one factor. On a RUSH project with little time, you should lean more on:
options: Thoroughly analyzing all the available data
questions.explanation: The factor is TIME (and resources). Rush / little time → lean more on your own knowledge and experience. Plenty of time + resources → be more data-driven. The ideal is always a blend.
questions.hint: Little time = no time to analyze everything, so you lean on…

title: Meet Claude
excerpt: The whole first module, testable: what Claude is, how to talk to it, and which of the three modes fits the job. 18 questions across Easy, Medium, and Hard — every objective covered, with the Cowork-vs-Chat trap I fell into built in on purpose.

questions.prompt: Claude's design is guided by three principles, often called the three H's. Which set names them?
questions.explanation: Claude is trained to be helpful, harmless, and honest — avoiding toxic/discriminatory outputs, refusing to aid illegal or unethical activity, and behaving truthfully. This is the backbone of its alignment.
questions.hint: Three H's: be useful, do no harm, tell the truth.

questions.prompt: The training approach that aligns Claude with human values and makes it operate transparently is called Constitutional ___.
questions.explanation: Constitutional AI is Anthropic's method for baking the helpful/harmless/honest values into Claude's training so it self-aligns to a set of principles.
questions.hint: A 'constitution' is a set of governing principles — for an artificial what?

questions.prompt: Claude answers by looking up pre-written answers from a database.
questions.explanation: Claude is a thought partner that GENERATES responses from learned patterns — it does not RETRIEVE canned answers from a lookup table. That generative, collaborative ability is what makes it more than a simple chatbot.
questions.hint: Composer, not librarian — it writes the answer, it doesn't fetch it.

questions.prompt: Select every surface through which Lesson 1 says you can access Claude.
questions.explanation: One Claude, many surfaces: web, desktop, and mobile apps, plus Claude Code, Claude in Slack, and Claude for Excel. Excel is the named Office surface — there is no 'Claude for PowerPoint' in the lesson.
questions.hint: Spreadsheets yes, slide decks no.

questions.prompt: In the Claude.ai interface, what does starting a 'new chat' from the sidebar do?
options: Begins a fresh conversation with no prior context
Loads your most recent conversation in full
questions.explanation: The sidebar lets you start new chats or jump back into old ones; a new chat is a fresh context. Projects add persistent context, and Artifacts turn outputs into shareable apps/tools.
questions.hint: New chat = blank slate, no memory of the last thread.

questions.prompt: Put the three parts of a strong prompt in the order the course presents them.
items: Setting the stage (your role, objective, context)
Defining the task (the action you want Claude to take)
questions.explanation: Stage → Task → Rules: first set the scene, then say what to do, then constrain how. The indie-streaming investor-deck prompt models all three.
questions.hint: Set the scene, give the order, lay down the rules.

questions.prompt: Per Lesson 2, uploading a relevant document or image to Claude is best described as…
options: A shortcut that gives Claude context to consider in its response
A way to permanently train Claude on your data
Something required before Claude will answer any question
A trick to make Claude respond faster
questions.explanation: Uploads (PDF, DOCX, CSV, TXT, PNG, JPEG) are a context shortcut — Claude parses the file so it understands your needs, but it does not permanently train on your data.
questions.hint: A file is a shortcut to context, not a training session.

questions.prompt: You want to refine one of your earlier messages rather than pile a new one on top. What does the course tell you to click?
options: The pencil (edit) icon on your message
questions.explanation: Click the pencil icon on any of your messages to edit and resubmit — refining the original prompt instead of stacking corrections on a derailed thread.
questions.hint: Pencil = rewrite the message, don't bolt on a new one.

questions.prompt: Claude's output came back too generic. According to the troubleshooting table, what's the first fix?
options: Add context — audience, role, and constraints
Just tell it to be shorter
questions.explanation: Generic output means Claude lacks context about YOUR situation. The fix is to add audience, role, and constraints — not change models or restart.
questions.hint: Bland output is starved for context — feed it the who and why.

questions.prompt: Select all four competencies of the 4D Framework for AI Fluency.
questions.explanation: The 4Ds are Delegation (what to hand off), Description (how to communicate the work), Discernment (judging the output), and Diligence (using AI responsibly). 'Determination' and 'Deployment' are decoys.
questions.hint: Hand it off, describe it, judge it, own it.

questions.prompt: The Stage / Task / Rules prompt framework lives inside which of the 4D competencies?
questions.explanation: Stage/Task/Rules is about communicating clearly with Claude — defining outputs and guiding the process — which is the Description competency. Discernment is evaluating the result; don't swap them.
questions.hint: Describing what you want = Description.

questions.prompt: Put the four steps of the simple (no-infrastructure) eval approach in the order the course gives them.
items: Gather 5-10 examples of a task you do regularly
Create test prompts that would generate similar outputs
Compare Claude's outputs against your known-good results
Refine the prompts (or flag where human review is essential)
questions.explanation: Evals = systematically testing Claude on tasks that matter to you: gather known examples, write test prompts, compare to known-good, then refine. It's rooted in Discernment.
questions.hint: Gather, prompt, compare, refine.

questions.prompt: In the desktop app, which mode is built for a quick back-and-forth exchange, brainstorm, or draft?
questions.explanation: Chat is the same Claude as claude.ai plus native extras (quick entry, dictation, screenshots) — optimized for quick exchanges and learning via dialogue. Cowork is for sustained deliverables; Code is for building software.
questions.hint: Quick chat = Chat. Big job = Cowork. Software = Code.

questions.prompt: You need Claude to read 50 signed contracts and produce a summary memo of every obligation — a big, autonomous deliverable. Which mode fits best?
questions.explanation: Cowork is agentic: give it a goal, connect resources, let it work through a large multi-source deliverable. The signal is the SIZE of the job, not the topic — it isn't coding, so Code is wrong; it's too big for a quick Chat.
questions.hint: Big autonomous deliverable → Cowork. Size of the job, not the topic.

questions.prompt: Cowork and Code run on the same engine — both are Claude Code underneath.
questions.explanation: Cowork and Code share the Claude Code engine: local, independent work, spinning up sub-agents and sustaining long tasks. Chat is the lighter, conversational surface.
questions.hint: Cowork + Code = one engine (Claude Code). Chat sits apart.

questions.prompt: Which feature set is unique to Code mode?
options: Visual diffs, built-in terminal, git versioning, and local vs. remote dev
questions.explanation: Code adds a full dev environment: Ask/Code/Plan, visual diffs, terminal, git, and local-vs-remote work. Quick entry/dictation/overlay belong to Chat; scheduled tasks and subagents belong to Cowork.
questions.hint: Diffs, terminal, git, local-or-remote = the builder's bench, Code.

questions.prompt: Once you've validated Claude against data you already understand, you've eliminated your responsibility to check its work.
questions.explanation: Validation builds confidence but never removes responsibility. You're still accountable for checking the numbers make sense and being transparent about AI's role — the non-negotiable of the Delegation-Diligence Loop.
questions.hint: Trust is earned, but the buck still stops with you.

questions.prompt: Explain the Delegation-Diligence Loop: why would you test Claude on a task where you ALREADY know the right answer?
questions.answer: Because you have a known-good result to measure Claude against. You pick a task you do regularly, reproduce a past analysis you already completed, and check Claude's output against the answer you already know. If it matches your known result you can trust it for similar future work; if you can't get there after several refinements you've learned this is a task you should not delegate. Validation builds confidence but never removes your responsibility to check the work.
questions.explanation: Testing against a known answer is the only way to measure trust objectively: match = delegate, repeated misses = don't. Rio's veterans-services attendance analysis anchors the idea — you need a concrete, scoped target you can grade against.
questions.hint: Grade Claude on an answer key you already hold — match it to trust it.
